headline: Manning and Texas ground game overwhelm Georgia in lopsided season opener
body: Arch Manning looked every bit the heir apparent in his first meaningful test, completing 21 of 35 passes for 252 yards and three touchdowns as Texas established complete control early and coasted to a 37-14 victory over Georgia. The performance was efficient rather than flashy, but it revealed something important about this Texas offense: when the running game works, Manning doesn't need to shoulder the load. He had time, space, and options all night.

The real story lived on the ground. Texas rushed for 245 yards with Gibson alone accounting for 128 on 15 carries, setting a tone that Georgia's defense never figured out how to disrupt. While Manning's three passing touchdowns grabbed headlines, it was the commitment to power running that broke this game open. Georgia managed just 58 rushing yards on 11 attempts, a disparity so stark it suggested a mismatch in physical identity rather than scheme.

Georgia quarterback Giles threw two touchdown passes to keep the scoreboard respectable, but his 11-of-25 completion rate and 161 total yards painted a picture of an offense starved for rhythm and rhythm for possessions. Texas forced Georgia into one-dimensional football early, and the Bulldogs never recovered. The turnover margin stayed close at 2-1 in Texas's favor, but field position and time of possession told the real tale: Texas held the ball for stretches, wore down a depleted Georgia front, and converted opportunities with precision.
turningPoint: Gibson's consistent 5-to-6-yard carries in the second quarter established Texas's run game dominance, forcing Georgia into predictable passing situations and surrendering the line of scrimmage.

unsungHero.name: Gibson
unsungHero.why: 128 rushing yards on 15 carries provided the platform for Manning's success and dictated tempo, yet rarely receives the visibility of a passing game's statistical package
